name: Pin / Unpin and Async Lifetimes
summary:
Library-level rather than language-level mechanism for address stability, needed because async desugars to self-referential state machines that cannot tolerate moves. Pin

is a wrapper proving the pointee won't move until dropped; Unpin is an opt-out auto-trait. Future::poll(self: Pin<&mut Self>, ...) threads the pin through every poll. The drop guarantee — once pinned, drop runs before the memory is reused — is what intrusive data structures actually rely on. Pin stabilized Rust 1.33 (February 2019); async/await 1.39 (November 2019).

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
**Pin/Unpin** is a library-level rather than language-level mechanism for *address stability*, needed because async desugars to self-referential state machines that cannot tolerate moves. `Pin

` is a wrapper proving the pointee won't move until dropped; `Unpin` is an opt-out auto-trait declaring "I don't care about address stability." `Future::poll(self: Pin<&mut Self>, ...)` threads the pin through every poll, making self-referential `async fn` state machines sound without compiler support.

sections.title: Drop guarantee and intrusive data structures
sections.content: The **drop guarantee** — once pinned, `drop` runs before the memory is reused — is what intrusive data structures (e.g. tokio's linked lists) actually rely on. Pin's safety contract is therefore not only about address stability during normal execution but also about destruction ordering.

sections.title: Why Pin is library-only
sections.content:
Pin stabilized in Rust 1.33 (February 2019); async/await in 1.39 (November 2019). The library-only character is unusual: Rust's ownership machinery is rich enough to express "addresses are stable until destruction" without a new language feature.

C++20 coroutines face the same self-reference problem and resolve it differently: coroutine frames are heap-allocated by default and accessed indirectly through `coroutine_handle`, sidestepping the address-stability question by making the frame's address fixed by allocation rather than by type discipline.
